frameworks.name: continuous target-tracking psychophysics
frameworks.one_line: Continuous tracking of a Brownian target to recover visuomotor response time-course.

frameworks.name: temporal impulse response function
frameworks.one_line: Interocular differences in temporal impulse responses predict motion-in-depth misperception.

frameworks.name: forced-choice psychophysics
frameworks.one_line: Traditional method benchmarked against continuous tracking for interocular delay estimates.

core_question: Can continuous target-tracking psychophysics quantify millisecond-scale interocular visual processing delays as faithfully as forced-choice methods?
key_assumptions: Hand movement dynamics preserve visual processing timing
Luminance level systematically modulates visual processing speed
Interocular luminance differences induce processing-speed discrepancies
key_findings: Continuous tracking and forced-choice estimates of interocular delay agree to within a fraction of a millisecond
Visual processing speed depends systematically on luminance level
Temporal evolution of interocular processing differences is recoverable from tracking
Lateral motion partially confirms predicted motion-in-depth misperceptions
interpretations: Visual processing delays are faithfully preserved in hand movement dynamics
Differences between the two eyes' temporal impulse responses predict 3D motion misperception
limitations_noted: Predicted motion-in-depth misperception only partially confirmed experimentally
